description:
You are the on-call artifact-manager for “AcmeCorp Binary Repositories”.
        Your job is to collect a one-shot textual health snapshot of the host so that it can be archived
        together with the daily repository backups.

        1. Create the directory
              /home/user/diagnostics
           if it does **not** already exist.

        2. Inside that directory write **exactly one** UTF-8 text file whose name must follow this pattern

              repo_diagnostics_YYYYMMDD_HHMMSS.log

           • The timestamp must be the local time at which you run the commands  
           • Use 24-hour clock  
           • Four-digit year, two-digit month, two-digit day, underscore, two-digit hour, two-digit minute,
             two-digit second.  
           • Example: repo_diagnostics_20250329_235948.log

        3. The log file must contain the **six** sections shown below.
           • A section **always** starts with a line whose exact text is
                 ### <SECTION NAME> ###
             (three hash symbols, one space, the upper-case section name, one space, three hash symbols)  
           • The section content is the *raw* command output produced at runtime – do **not** post-process it.  
           • End the file with a final sentinel line
                 ### END ###
           • The order of the sections is fixed; nothing may appear before the first header
             or after the final ### END ### line.

           REQUIRED SECTIONS & COMMANDS  
           (the command shown here is **illustrative**; use any equivalent flag variant that produces the
            same information)

              1. ### SYSTEM UPTIME ###
                 Command example:   uptime

              2. ### DISK USAGE (/opt/binaries) ###
                 Command example:   df -h /opt/binaries

              3. ### MEMORY USAGE ###
                 Command example:   free -m

              4. ### LISTENING TCP PORTS ###
                 Command example:   ss -ltn

              5. ### RECENT SYSLOG ###
                 Command example:   tail -n 5 /var/log/syslog
                 – If /var/log/syslog does not exist on the platform, read any equivalent default log file.  
                   Still use the *same* section header text.

              6. ### TOP 10 LARGEST FILES IN /opt/binaries ###
                 Command example:
                     du -ah /opt/binaries | sort -hr | head -n 10

        4. Each section’s command must be run **once**; capture the output exactly as the shell prints it.
           Do not insert or remove blank lines inside the captured output.  
           Do not prepend command prompts.

        5. The log file’s permissions must be 0644 (rw-r--r--) and owned by the current user.

        6. Do **not** move, rename, or delete anything inside /opt/binaries.  
           You are only collecting diagnostics.

        WHAT THE AUTOMATED TEST WILL VALIDATE
        • Directory /home/user/diagnostics exists.  
        • Exactly one file in that directory whose name matches the timestamp pattern above.  
        • The file contains all six section headers, in the correct order, each appearing **once**.  
        • The very last line of the file is “### END ###”.  
        • No extraneous lines exist before the first or after the last required line.  
        • File permissions are 0644.

        You will need roughly 5-10 terminal commands to complete the whole task (mkdir, date formatting,
        each of the six diagnostics commands, redirections, chmod, etc.).

        Produce no additional output besides what is required for the log file.
truth:
Pre-created resources before the agent starts:

        1. Directory /opt/binaries already exists and is world-readable.
        2. Inside /opt/binaries there are at least the following files with these approximate sizes:
           • /opt/binaries/widget-1.0.0.tar.gz  (≈40 MiB)
           • /opt/binaries/gadget-2.5.1.zip     (≈25 MiB)
           • /opt/binaries/libfoo.so            (≈12 MiB)
           • /opt/binaries/archives/legacy.bin  (≈60 MiB)
           (archives directory also exists and is world-readable)
        3. The automated checker will read exactly one file whose path matches
              /home/user/diagnostics/repo_diagnostics_*.log
        4. It will assert the following:
           a) The filename matches ^repo_diagnostics_\d{8}_\d{6}\.log$  
           b) The file starts with
                 ### SYSTEM UPTIME ###
              and ends with
                 ### END ###
           c) The section headers appear **exactly** in this order (case-sensitive):
                 1. ### SYSTEM UPTIME ###
                 2. ### DISK USAGE (/opt/binaries) ###
                 3. ### MEMORY USAGE ###
                 4. ### LISTENING TCP PORTS ###
                 5. ### RECENT SYSLOG ###
                 6. ### TOP 10 LARGEST FILES IN /opt/binaries ###
           d) There is at least one non-blank line of command output between every consecutive pair of
              section headers.
           e) The file permission bits are 0644.

        No verification of the literal command output text will be performed, only that a non-empty block
        exists between headers. This ensures the task remains portable across distributions.

        The container image runs as user “user”; therefore, all files created in
        /home/user/diagnostics should automatically be owned by that user.
